hand.title: Daily Hand

events.text: Your cards — 3, 3. You kicked off the action with a raise, and your opponent decided to call, setting the stage for a thrilling showdown.
Flop — 9, 10, Ace. You decided to bet, trying to assert your dominance, and your opponent chose to call, still in the hunt.
Turn — Jack. You checked, passing the action to your opponent, who then bet — a bigger wager than expected, signaling a potential shift in momentum.
River — 10. Your opponent fired again for value, making a second consecutive bet, as the board paired, likely strengthening their hand.

wrongReasons: A 8 has similar strength, but look at when the aggression shifted — the turn card connects with the correct hand in a way it doesn't connect with A 8.
10 8 makes a three of a kind here — a hand that strong would have raised earlier and pushed harder for maximum value. The measured sizing in this hand points to something weaker.
3 5 doesn't benefit from the turn card the way the correct hand does. A player holding only one pair wouldn't have the confidence to raise on that street.
hand.explanation:
Answer: 6 J

Your opponent held strong with a high card on the flop, then landed a crucial pair on the turn with the Jack, and ultimately made two pair on the river, as the 10 paired, explaining their sudden aggression on the turn and river.
